stem: A hospital uses an anti-CCP antibody test to screen for rheumatoid arthritis. The test has a sensitivity of 84% and a specificity of 78%. The prevalence of rheumatoid arthritis in the patient population is estimated to be between 7.3% and 16.2%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has rheumatoid arthritis? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(rheumatoid arthritis | positive test) ranges from 0.231 to 0.425 as prevalence varies from 7.3% to 16.2%. Point estimate at 11.7% prevalence: 0.336.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.22.
The confidence interval [0.231, 0.425] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (84%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
